schools.summary: Paul bids the Corinthians be 'followers' (imitators, mimetai) of him — to copy his example, especially the self-denying love for others' good he had just described (10:33) — but only so far as he himself imitates Christ, the supreme pattern, Paul a model only as he conforms to him (Barnes, Robertson).
Tertullian (with Henry): ministers may be imitated, but always within the measure of their following Christ; the ultimate example is the Lord himself.

themes: Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ
the call to imitate Paul as he imitates Christ
Christ the supreme pattern
imitation of teachers bounded

schools.summary: Paul, before correcting their faults, praises the Corinthians for remembering him and holding fast what he had taught; 'the ordinances' (paradoseis, traditions, instructions) being the teachings and practices Paul had delivered — apostolic instructions concerning faith and worship, to be retained as he gave them (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry): Paul commends what is praiseworthy before reproving the disorders that follow; the 'traditions' here are apostolic, not later human inventions.

themes: ye remember me in all things, and keep the ordinances
Paul's praise for keeping the traditions
the apostolic ordinances delivered
praise before reproof

schools.summary: Paul lays down a graduated order of 'headship' (kephale, denoting authority, source, preeminence): Christ is the head of every man (his Lord and source), the man the head of the woman (an order of authority and precedence grounded in creation, vv.8-9, not a denial of equal worth), and God the head of Christ (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: 'the head of Christ is God' refers to Christ in his mediatorial office, his voluntary subordination to the Father in the economy of redemption — not a denial of his equal deity, but the order within the Godhead's saving work.

themes: the head of every man is Christ; and the head of the woman is the man
the head of Christ is God
the order of headship
authority and precedence in worship

schools.summary: Paul applies the headship order: a man who prays or prophesies (leads in public worship) with his head covered (veiled) dishonours 'his head' — both his own head and, figuratively, Christ his head; for to be veiled was, in that culture, a sign of subjection or inappropriate to his place, the uncovered head befitting his position under Christ alone (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the outward sign was to accord with the order of headship; a man veiled would obscure the dignity God gave him as the image and glory of God (v.7).

themes: Every man praying or prophesying, having his head covered, dishonoureth his head
the man covering his head dishonours his head
the uncovered head befitting his place
the sign accords with the order

schools.summary: A woman who prays or prophesies publicly with her head uncovered (unveiled) dishonours 'her head' — herself and her husband, the man being her head; for to appear unveiled was, in that culture, as shameful as if her head were shaved, a mark of disgrace, immodesty, or scandal (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the veil was the recognized sign of the woman's modesty and her place under the man; to cast it off was to overturn the order God had set and to bring shame.

themes: every woman that prayeth or prophesieth with her head uncovered dishonoureth her head
as if she were shaven
the woman uncovered dishonours her head
the veil the sign of modesty

schools.summary: Paul presses the point by a reductio: if a woman will not be covered (veiled), let her be consistent and also be shorn (have her hair cut off), for the unveiled head and the shorn head are of a piece; but since it is plainly shameful for a woman to be shorn or shaven, it is likewise shameful to be unveiled, so she ought to be covered (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: the woman's own sense of shame at being shorn proves the impropriety of being unveiled; the veil and the hair belong together as marks of her modesty.

themes: if the woman be not covered, let her also be shorn
if it be a shame for a woman to be shorn or shaven, let her be covered
the argument from shame
the veil bound to modesty

schools.summary: The man ought not to veil his head because he is 'the image and glory of God' — bearing God's authority and reflecting his glory in his headship, which veiling would obscure; the woman being 'the glory of the man,' made from him and for him (vv.8-9), reflecting his honour, her veiling acknowledging this relation (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: this is an order, not an inferiority of nature — both man and woman bear God's image as to the soul, but in the order of headship the man immediately reflects God's authority, the woman through the man.

themes: a man... is the image and glory of God
the woman is the glory of the man
the man God's image and glory
the order of headship from creation

schools.summary: Paul grounds the order in creation: the man was not made from the woman, but the woman from the man — referring to Eve's formation from Adam's side (Genesis 2:21-22); this priority of origin establishing a certain precedence of the man, the woman deriving her being from him (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): this priority grounds the order of headship, but (as vv.11-12 will balance) does not make the woman of less worth or independent of the man.

themes: the man is not of the woman; but the woman of the man
the woman's origin from the man
Eve formed from Adam
the creational ground of the order

schools.summary: The second creational reason: the man was not created for the woman's sake, but the woman for the man's — made as a helper suited to him (Genesis 2:18), to be his companion and aid; the end of her creation confirming the order of headship, she being made with reference to him, though balanced by the mutual dependence of the sexes (vv.11-12, Barnes, Robertson).

themes: neither was the man created for the woman; but the woman for the man
the woman created for the man's sake
a help meet for him
the order confirmed by her creation

schools.summary: Most take 'power' (exousia) by metonymy for the veil — the sign or token of the authority (of the man) to which she is subject (Robertson: a symbol of authority, more a sign of subjection); but Ellicott objects that exousia properly means one's own power and so reads it as a sign of the woman's own dignity (her hair, her glory). Barnes and Clarke frankly confess the phrase is among the most obscure in all Scripture, its meaning to them inexplicable.

On 'because of the angels': most (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley, Ellicott) take the angels as the good angels present in worship, witnesses of the assembly, who would be shocked at indecency or disorder (cf. Hebrews 1:14; the angels veil their faces before Jehovah, Isaiah 6:2); Ellicott also notes the Genesis 6 reading (fallen angels), and Lightfoot suggests human messengers or ministers.

Calvin: 'power' is by metonymy a token that she is under authority — a covering, whether veil, hood, or kerchief; and the angels are in attendance upon Christ their head and minister to him, so that when women usurp the token of authority they make their baseness manifest before the angels of heaven.

debates.question: What is the 'power on her head,' and who are the 'angels'?

sides.position: 'Power' = the veil, sign of the authority she is under; the 'angels' = good angels present in worship
'Power' = a sign of the woman's own dignity (her hair); 'angels' = good angels (Ellicott also notes the Genesis 6 fallen-angels reading)
The phrase is frankly inexplicable / obscure

outliers.note: Suggests 'power' may mean the woman's own power in her hand, and the 'angels' may be human messengers or even, in this epistle's usage, devils (1 Cor 6:3).

themes: the woman ought to have power on her head because of the angels
the woman's covering a sign of authority
the angels present in worship
the hardest crux of the chapter

schools.summary: Paul, having stressed the woman's subordination, now balances it: lest the man assume too much, neither sex is independent of the other — each is necessary to the other's good, the two formed for union; 'in the Lord,' by the Lord's arrangement in the Christian economy, the sexes are mutually dependent, neither independent nor superior in worth (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): this checks the man's pride and consoles the woman — the man is but half the race, owing his being (by birth) to the woman; in the Lord Jesus there is neither male nor female as to worth, neither excluded nor preferred in his kingdom.

themes: neither is the man without the woman, neither the woman without the man, in the Lord
the mutual dependence of the sexes
the check on the man's pride
equal worth in the Lord

schools.summary: Paul balances the creation-order: as the first woman was made from the man, so every man since is born 'by the woman' — each man comes into existence through a mother; and he traces all to God, both man and woman and all things being ultimately 'of God,' the common source of both (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: this both humbles the man (who owes his birth to the woman) and reconciles all to God's appointment; both sexes are to accept with humility the place God has assigned them.

themes: as the woman is of the man, even so is the man also by the woman
but all things of God
the reciprocal origin of the sexes
all things of God

schools.summary: Paul now turns from argument to their own judgment: 'judge in (among) yourselves' — appeal to your own sense of what is fitting; is it 'comely' (decent, becoming) for a woman to pray to God unveiled? inviting them to consult the established sense of propriety, by which praying unveiled would be indecorous (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: the matter is so plain that Paul is content to leave it to their own discernment of what is seemly in the presence of God.

themes: Judge in yourselves: is it comely that a woman pray unto God uncovered
the appeal to their sense of propriety
what is comely in worship
a plain case left to their judgment

schools.summary: 'Nature' here means a deep internal sense of propriety expressed in prevailing or universal custom — what men generally feel to be fitting (Barnes); for a man to wear long hair (effeminately cultivated) was generally regarded as shameful, unmanly, the mark of the female, the universal sense of fitness condemning it; though some take 'nature' as mere long custom among Jews and Greeks (Gill).

Calvin (with Edwards): 'nature' means nature in the proper sense, not mere custom — an innate sense that it is a shame for a man to appear with the established signs of the female sex; though some ancient nations wore long hair, the general and natural sense remains that flowing hair befits the woman, not the man.

themes: Doth not even nature itself teach you, that, if a man have long hair, it is a shame
the witness of nature
long hair a shame to a man
nature proper or custom

schools.summary: The same nature that makes long hair shameful in a man makes it a 'glory' (an ornament, honour, beauty) in a woman — her flowing hair adorns and becomes her; and her hair is given her (by nature, by God) 'for a covering,' a natural veil, confirming the propriety of the artificial veil, both signifying the same modesty and order (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

themes: if a woman have long hair, it is a glory to her
her hair is given her for a covering
the woman's hair her glory and natural covering
nature confirming the practice

schools.summary: Paul, having reasoned the matter, anticipates that some might still dispute: if anyone is minded to be contentious (quarrelsome) about it, he cuts off further debate by appealing to the universal practice — 'we' (the apostles) and all 'the churches of God' have no such custom (of women praying unveiled, or of contentiousness on this point), the settled usage silencing the contentious (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: where Scripture and reason have spoken, the universal practice of the churches confirms the matter, and contentiousness against it is to be refused; decency and order are to be observed.

themes: if any man seem to be contentious, we have no such custom, neither the churches of God
the appeal to universal custom
the practice of the churches of God
contention silenced by order

schools.summary: Paul turns from the praise of v.2 to blame: in the matter he is about to address (the Lord's Supper), he cannot praise them but the contrary; the grave charge being that their very assemblies, which ought to edify, were doing harm — coming together 'for the worse,' made the occasion of sin and division rather than of good (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): it is a sad thing when the public assembly, meant for edification and worship, becomes through disorder a means of injury; the Corinthian gatherings had so degenerated.

themes: in this that I declare unto you I praise you not
ye come together not for the better, but for the worse
blame for harmful assemblies
the assembly degenerated

schools.summary: Paul names the first disorder: when they assemble 'in the church' (as a congregation), there are 'divisions' (schismata, splits, factions) among them — not now the party-divisions of ch.1, but the social splits manifested at the common meal; and Paul 'partly believes' the report, neither dismissing it nor accepting every detail, a charitable yet serious reckoning (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: divisions in the very assembly, and at the sacred meal, were a grievous evil, contradicting the unity the Supper signifies.

themes: when ye come together in the church, I hear that there be divisions among you
I partly believe it
the report of divisions
factions in the assembly

schools.summary: 'Heresies' (haireseis) here means not doctrinal heresies in the later sense but factions, parties, divisions of opinion; 'there must be' indicates not divine approval but the certainty, given human nature, that such will arise; God overrules them so that the 'approved' (dokimoi, the genuine, tested believers) are made manifest, distinguished from the unsound (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God permits divisions to sift the church, that the sincere may be proved and shown; the evil of faction is overruled to a good end, though the faction itself is blameworthy.

themes: there must be also heresies among you
that they which are approved may be made manifest
factions manifesting the approved
God overruling division for good

schools.summary: Paul declares that, given their disorders, when they assemble it is 'not to eat the Lord's supper' — their manner of observance so corrupted it that it could not be called the Lord's Supper at all; the sacred meal instituted by the Lord emptied of its character by their selfish, divided feasting (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: where the institution of Christ is so perverted — where there is no true communion, but selfish indulgence — the name of the Lord's Supper is laid claim to in vain.

themes: when ye come together therefore into one place, this is not to eat the Lord's supper
the disorderly meal not the Lord's Supper
the sacrament emptied by abuse
the institution perverted

schools.summary: The disorder: instead of a true common meal (the love-feast joined to the Supper), each ate his own provisions greedily, not waiting for or sharing with others — the rich consuming what they brought while the poor had nothing; the shameful result being that some went hungry while others ate and drank to excess, even to drunkenness (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the sacred meal, meant to express love and equality among believers, was turned into an occasion of selfishness, gluttony, and contempt for the poor.

themes: every one taketh before other his own supper
one is hungry, and another is drunken
the selfish disorder at the meal
the Supper profaned by selfishness

schools.summary: Paul rebukes the confusion of the sacred and the common: if they merely wish to satisfy hunger, they have their own homes for that, the assembly not being for private feasting; their conduct despised (held in contempt) the church of God and shamed the poor ('them that have not'), humiliating the needy by their selfish excess — for which Paul withholds all praise (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): their conduct is wholly to be condemned; Paul gives no praise, for they have turned the sacred assembly into an occasion of contempt for the church and shame to the poor.

themes: have ye not houses to eat and to drink in
despise ye the church of God, and shame them that have not
the rebuke of contempt for the church and poor
no praise for their abuse

schools.summary: Paul grounds the account in direct revelation: he received the institution of the Supper 'of the Lord' (by direct revelation from Christ, or through the apostolic tradition tracing to him) and faithfully delivered it to them; the touching detail that it was on the very night of his betrayal, in the shadow of the cross, that the Lord took bread, heightening its solemnity (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Cyril (with Calvin): the Supper rests on Christ's own institution and Paul's faithful transmission; its observance is to conform to what the Lord appointed, not to human invention.

themes: I have received of the Lord that which also I delivered unto you
the Lord Jesus the same night in which he was betrayed took bread
the institution received from the Lord
the Supper grounded in Christ's appointment

schools.summary: Christ gave thanks (eucharistesas, whence 'Eucharist') and broke the bread; 'this is my body' is figurative — 'this is' means 'this represents' or 'is the sign of' my body (as 'this cup is the new testament,' the bread is the emblem). Robertson notes 'broken' is not genuine in the text and the body itself was not broken (John 19:36); the Supper is a memorial (anamnesis) appointed to keep Christ's death in remembrance (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the expression is figurative (sacramental metonymy, the sign given the name of the thing signified, as the Spirit was called a dove), yet the reality is conjoined with the sign — not an empty representation; the soul truly partakes of Christ's body by the secret virtue of the Spirit, who joins things separated by distance, while Christ remains in heaven; against both the Papist transubstantiation and a bare memorial.
Cyril: literal — 'Since He Himself declared and said of the Bread, This is My Body, who shall dare to doubt any longer? and since He affirmed, This is My Blood, who shall hesitate?' (real presence).

Wesley: 'this is my body' means 'this broken bread is the sign of my body, which is even now to be wounded for your iniquities'; take and eat in humble, thankful, obediential remembrance of his dying love.

debates.question: Does 'this is my body' teach the real presence, a figurative sign, or a spiritual real participation?

sides.position: Literal — the bread is Christ's body (real presence)
Figurative — the bread represents or is the sign of his body (memorial)
Figurative expression, but the reality conjoined with the sign — the soul truly partakes of Christ's body by the Spirit (spiritual real presence)

themes: this is my body, which is broken for you
this do in remembrance of me
the words over the bread
the eucharistic crux

schools.summary: Christ took the cup 'after supper' (after the paschal meal), distinguishing the Supper from a common meal and the passover it followed; 'this cup is the new testament (covenant, diatheke) in my blood' — the cup the symbol and pledge of the new covenant ratified in Christ's blood, as the old was sealed with sacrificial blood (Exodus 24:8); a memorial to be observed, without fixed frequency, in remembrance of him (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): the Supper is both a covenant and a confirmatory pledge of the covenant — the sacraments are seals of the divine will, helps to faith; the cup, like the bread, given to all (against the Papist withholding of the cup), in remembrance of Christ's blood poured out for reconciliation.

themes: This cup is the new testament in my blood
this do ye, as oft as ye drink it, in remembrance of me
the new covenant in his blood
the cup a memorial and pledge

schools.summary: The repeated observance of the Supper (bread and cup together) is in view, its frequency left to the church but its character fixed; 'ye do shew' (kataggellete, proclaim, announce) the Lord's death — the Supper a public proclamation, a setting forth of Christ's death, not a bare memory but a declaration of the atonement; to be observed 'till he come,' a perpetual ordinance for the whole gospel age (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the Supper looks forward to Christ's second coming, when the sign gives way to sight; it is a perpetual proclamation of his death until that day.

themes: as often as ye eat this bread, and drink this cup, ye do shew the Lord's death
till he come
the Supper a proclamation of Christ's death
the perpetual ordinance till his return

schools.summary: To partake 'unworthily' (anaxios) means in an irreverent, unfitting manner — as the Corinthians did, with selfishness, divisions, and contempt for the sacred meal; it refers to the manner of partaking, not to personal unworthiness in general (else none could partake); such a one is 'guilty of the body and blood of the Lord,' profaning the sacred symbols and so offending against Christ himself (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: to handle the sacred symbols irreverently is to insult Christ crucified; the warning is against profaning the ordinance, not against approaching with a humble sense of one's own unworthiness.

themes: whosoever shall eat this bread, and drink this cup of the Lord, unworthily
shall be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord
the guilt of unworthy partaking
profaning the sacred symbols

schools.summary: The prescribed safeguard: each is to 'examine' (dokimazeto, prove, test, scrutinize) himself — his heart, his understanding of the ordinance, his faith and repentance — before approaching the Supper, and having so examined himself, he is to eat; self-examination is not meant to keep the worthy away, but to prepare them to partake rightly and reverently (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Chrysostom): the call is not to a perfection that would exclude all, but to a sincere self-scrutiny that one come with faith, repentance, and love, discerning the sacred nature of the act.

themes: let a man examine himself, and so let him eat
the duty of self-examination
proving the heart before partaking
preparation, not exclusion

schools.summary: 'Damnation' (krima) is too strong; it means judgment, condemnation, or chastisement, and here (per vv.30-32) temporal judgment — weakness, sickness, death — a chastening to save from final condemnation, NOT eternal damnation. 'Not discerning the Lord's body' means not discriminating between the sacred bread (the symbol of Christ's body) and common food — the Corinthians' very fault — regarding the Supper as an ordinary meal (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

Ellicott: 'the Lord's' is a textual addition; 'the body' here means the Church — he who eats without discerning the Body (the corporate church) in the assembly eats judgment to himself; the fault being the failure to regard the gathering as a corporate meeting of the one Body, which alone fits the context and the remedy of vv.33-34.

Calvin (with Wesley): they distinguish not the Lord's body as a sacred thing from the profane, handling it irreverently; they pay the penalty of so dreadful a profanation — yet the judgment is the chastening of the Lord, that they be not condemned with the world (v.32).

debates.question: Does 'not discerning the body' mean failing to distinguish the sacred elements (Christ's body) or failing to discern the Church (the corporate body)?

sides.position: The sacred elements — not distinguishing the bread, the symbol of Christ's body, from common food
The Church — not discerning the corporate Body in the assembly ('the Lord's' being a textual addition)

themes: he that eateth and drinketh unworthily, eateth and drinketh damnation to himself
not discerning the Lord's body
the judgment on unworthy partaking
damnation as temporal chastening

schools.summary: Paul gives concrete evidence of the judgment: 'for this cause' (on account of their unworthy partaking) God had visited them with chastisements — many among them being 'weak' (feeble in health) and 'sickly' (diseased), and 'many sleep' (had died, 'sleep' a gentle term for the death of believers); these temporal calamities were God's chastening for their profanation of the Supper (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God had actually inflicted bodily judgments — sickness and death — to mark his displeasure at their abuse and to call them to reverence; the chastening confirms the warning of v.29.

themes: for this cause many are weak and sickly among you, and many sleep
the temporal judgments on the Corinthians
weakness, sickness, and death
God's chastening for the abuse

schools.summary: Paul states the way of escape: 'if we would judge ourselves' (examine and condemn our own faults, set ourselves right) 'we should not be judged' (chastened) by God — self-judgment forestalls divine judgment; the wordplay (diakrino/krino) marking that if we discriminatingly judge ourselves, we escape being chastened by the Lord (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God's chastening comes where self-examination is neglected; were we duly to judge and correct ourselves, the rod would be stayed — the remedy lies in honest self-judgment.

themes: if we would judge ourselves, we should not be judged
self-judgment averts God's judgment
self-examination forestalling chastening
the way of escape

schools.summary: The 'judgment' on believers (the sickness and death of v.30) is 'chastening' (paideuometha, fatherly discipline, correction) from the Lord — not the condemnation of an angry judge but the discipline of a loving Father (Hebrews 12); its gracious purpose being that believers 'should not be condemned with the world,' the temporal discipline correcting and preserving them from the final condemnation of the ungodly (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the chastening of believers and the condemnation of the world are wholly different — the former corrective and saving, the latter punitive and destroying.

themes: when we are judged, we are chastened of the Lord
that we should not be condemned with the world
God's judgment of believers fatherly chastening
chastening unto salvation

schools.summary: Paul draws the practical lesson from all the foregoing: 'when ye come together to eat' (for the Supper and the love-feast), 'tarry one for another' (wait for one another) — the cure for their selfish disorder (v.21); the simple remedy of mutual consideration, that instead of each greedily consuming his own provision, they wait so that all may partake together in love and equality (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the unity and charity the Supper signifies are to be expressed in the very manner of coming together; waiting for one another restores the common, brotherly character of the meal.

themes: when ye come together to eat, tarry one for another
the practical remedy of waiting
mutual consideration at the meal
charity restoring the common meal

schools.summary: Paul's plain instruction: if anyone is hungry, let him satisfy his appetite at home — so that the assembly is for the sacred ordinance, not for satisfying hunger; this separates the common meal from the Supper, that their coming together may not result in (chastening) condemnation, as it had (vv.29-30); right order would avert the judgment (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: other lesser matters of order Paul will arrange when he visits them in person; he addresses now only the most pressing disorders of the assembly.

themes: if any man hunger, let him eat at home
that ye come not together unto condemnation
the rest will I set in order when I come
the order that averts condemnation
